event: Early hominins inhabit the Azykh Cave in Karabakh, leaving some of the oldest evidence of human presence in the Caucasus.
Neolithic communities settle the fertile plains around the Kura and Araxes rivers, developing early agriculture.
The Leyla-Tepe and later Kura-Araxes cultures flourish, marking advances in metallurgy and settled village life.
Bronze Age cultures produce the rock carvings of Gobustan, depicting hunting, dancing, and reed boats.
The kingdom of Mannae emerges south of Lake Urmia, becoming a regional power between Assyria and Urartu.
The region falls under the Achaemenid Persian Empire, organized into satrapies.
Alexander the Great's conquest of Persia ends Achaemenid rule, and the satrap Atropates establishes Atropatene in the south.
Caucasian Albania, a kingdom in the north, clashes with the Roman general Pompey during his campaigns in the Caucasus.
Caucasian Albania becomes a vassal of the Sasanian Persian Empire.
Caucasian Albania adopts Christianity as a state religion, establishing its own autocephalous church.
Arab Muslim armies invade the region following the Battle of Nahavand, beginning the spread of Islam.
The Umayyad Caliphate fully absorbs Caucasian Albania, accelerating Islamization and Arab settlement.
Babak Khorramdin launches the Khurramite rebellion against Abbasid rule, resisting for over two decades.
The Shirvanshah dynasty establishes itself at Shirvan, ruling much of the region for centuries.
The Eldiguzid (Atabegs of Azerbaijan) dynasty rises to power, governing on behalf of the Seljuk sultans.
The poet Nizami Ganjavi, author of the Khamsa, flourishes in Ganja, shaping Persian literary culture.
Mongol invasions devastate the region, which is incorporated into the Ilkhanate.
Timur (Tamerlane) invades and subjugates the lands of Azerbaijan during his Caucasus campaigns.
Shah Ismail I, founder of the Safavid dynasty, establishes Shia Islam as the state religion across the region.
The Safavids end the Shirvanshah dynasty and bring Shirvan under direct rule.
Following Nader Shah's assassination, numerous semi-independent khanates emerge, including Baku, Shaki, Karabakh, and Quba.
The Treaty of Gulistan cedes most northern khanates from Persia to the Russian Empire after the first Russo-Persian War.
The Treaty of Turkmenchay finalizes Russian annexation of the territory, fixing the Araxes River as the border with Persia.
The first oil well in the world is mechanically drilled at Bibi-Heybat near Baku, predating American drilling.
Russia abolishes the oil monopoly system, sparking the Baku oil boom that draws investors like the Nobel brothers and Rothschilds.
Baku produces roughly half the world's oil, becoming a global petroleum capital.
Armenian-Tatar (Azerbaijani) clashes erupt across the Caucasus amid revolutionary turmoil in the Russian Empire.
The Azerbaijan Democratic Republic is proclaimed in May, becoming the first secular democratic republic in the Muslim world.
Azerbaijani women gain the right to vote, among the earliest enfranchisements in the Muslim world.
The Red Army invades and the Azerbaijan Democratic Republic falls, establishing Soviet rule.
Azerbaijan joins the Soviet Union as part of the Transcaucasian SFSR.
The Nagorno-Karabakh Autonomous Oblast is created within Soviet Azerbaijan, with a majority Armenian population.
Azerbaijan becomes a full constituent republic of the USSR as the Azerbaijan SSR.
Stalinist purges devastate the Azerbaijani intelligentsia and political leadership.
Baku's oilfields become a vital fuel source for the Soviet war effort and a key German objective in World War II.
The Nagorno-Karabakh conflict reignites as the enclave votes to join Armenia, triggering intercommunal violence.
Soviet troops crack down on nationalist protests in Baku in the deadly 'Black January' events.
Azerbaijan declares independence from the Soviet Union as the USSR collapses.
Azerbaijan joins the United Nations and full-scale war with Armenia over Nagorno-Karabakh intensifies.
Heydar Aliyev returns to power as president amid political instability and military setbacks.
A Russian-brokered ceasefire halts the First Nagorno-Karabakh War, leaving Armenia in control of the enclave and surrounding districts.
Azerbaijan signs the 'Contract of the Century' with international oil companies to develop Caspian offshore fields.
Ilham Aliyev succeeds his father Heydar Aliyev as president, beginning a long family-led tenure.
The Baku-Tbilisi-Ceyhan oil pipeline opens, exporting Caspian crude to world markets via Turkey.
Baku hosts the Eurovision Song Contest, showcasing the capital's oil-funded modernization.
Baku hosts the inaugural European Games, a major international multi-sport event.
The Second Nagorno-Karabakh War sees Azerbaijan recapture most of the disputed territory in 44 days of fighting.
Azerbaijan launches a swift offensive that dissolves the breakaway Nagorno-Karabakh administration and prompts an Armenian exodus.
Azerbaijan hosts the COP29 United Nations climate summit in Baku.
